title: World Series Betting

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-05-stochastic-processes.md

setup.narrative: A best-of-7 series. You want to engineer a SCHEDULE OF BETS such that you net exactly +$100 if Team A wins the series and -$100 if Team B wins. Each bet is double-or-nothing (winner takes the wager).
rules: State (i, j) = A has won i games, B has won j games. Series ends at i = 4 or j = 4.
f(i, j) = your net WHEN AT state (i, j), under optimal betting going forward.
f(4, j) = +100 (A wins series). f(i, 4) = -100 (B wins). At (i, j) with i, j < 4, you place bet y and end up at (i+1, j) with payoff f + y or (i, j+1) with payoff f - y.
setup.question: Find f(0, 0) and the bet at each state. (Particularly, what's the bet at the start?)

learningObjectives: Set up the recursion: y(i, j) = (f(i+1, j) - f(i, j+1)) / 2 and f(i, j) = (f(i+1, j) + f(i, j+1)) / 2.
Solve backward from the absorbing boundaries (+/- 100).
Recognize the structural identity with binomial-tree option pricing.

stages.label: Set up the bet recursion
stages.prompt: At state (i, j), you place bet y. After the next game, your net is either f(i, j) + y (A wins) or f(i, j) - y (B wins). For the recursion to satisfy f(i+1, j) = f(i, j) + y AND f(i, j+1) = f(i, j) - y, what are y and f(i, j)?
stages.explanation: Adding the two equations: f(i+1, j) + f(i, j+1) = 2 * f(i, j), so f(i, j) is the AVERAGE of its successors. Subtracting: f(i+1, j) - f(i, j+1) = 2y, so y is HALF the gap between successors.

stages.label: Solve a few back-states
stages.prompt: f(3, 3): one more game decides. Successors: f(4, 3) = +100, f(3, 4) = -100. So f(3, 3) = ?  And the bet?
stages.explanation: Average of +100 and -100 is 0. The bet to bridge from 0 to +100 (or -100) is 100. Risk it all at the deciding game.

stages.label: Two more back-states
stages.explanation: Average of successors gives the value; half the gap gives the bet. f(3, 2) = +50; f(2, 3) = -50. Both bets are $50.

stages.label: Commit f(0, 0)
stages.prompt: By the symmetry f(i, j) = -f(j, i), f(0, 0) = 0. The bet at (0, 0) is y(0, 0) = (f(1, 0) - f(0, 1))/2. By symmetry f(1, 0) = +x, f(0, 1) = -x, so y(0, 0) = x. Computing x via backward induction (or recognizing it as the binomial-tree price of a +100/-100 binary on 7 trials): x = ?
stages.explanation: Detailed back-induction yields x = 31.25 (i.e., the initial bet is $31.25). Equivalently, this is the binomial-tree DELTA at the start for a digital option with payoff +/- 100 expiring after the series concludes.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: Why is f(0, 0) = 0? What general principle (martingale of the betting strategy) makes this true?
Compute the bet at every state from (0, 0) outward. There are at most 7 distinct future games -- how many distinct bet sizes appear?
Connect to options pricing: Black-Scholes pricing IS continuous-time replication via dynamic hedging.
